title: Dissipation-Selected Resonant Fronts in a Driven-Dissipative Bose-Hubbard Lattice
abstract: Spatially structured dissipation organizes driven quantum matter beyond Hamiltonian control. We show that a dissipation gradient combined with a Stark-induced detuning ramp selects a nonlinear resonance slice in a two-dimensional driven-dissipative Bose-Hubbard lattice, producing a pinned density front in generalized Gross-Pitaevskii simulations. The underlying resonance condition fixes the front position, while its Airy-like profile obeys a width scaling set by tunneling stiffness and the effective detuning slope. Treating the front as an emergent interface explains how tuning the selected resonance toward the minimum-loss side yields Peierls-Nabarro depinning steps, discrete transverse pattern locking, spatiotemporal chaos, and minimum-loss localization. Center-of-mass and generalized-imbalance diagnostics map these outcomes into a dynamical phase diagram as detuning-ramp slope and dissipation-gradient strength vary. The results suggest structured dissipation as a mechanism for reconfigurable transport barriers and nonequilibrium interfaces in programmable bosonic lattices.
summary: This paper shows that spatially structured dissipation can be used to select and control nonlinear resonant fronts in a driven-dissipative Bose-Hubbard lattice. By tuning the dissipation gradient and detuning ramp, one can navigate between pinned, chaotic, and localized phases. This provides a mechanism for creating reconfigurable transport barriers in programmable quantum simulators.
main_problem: Investigating how spatially structured dissipation and a Stark-induced detuning ramp can be used to organize driven quantum matter and create reconfigurable transport barriers or nonequilibrium interfaces.
main_result: The study identifies a mechanism where a nonlinear resonance condition fixes a pinned density front, which can transition into pattern-locked states, spatiotemporal chaos, or localized states depending on the dissipation gradient and detuning slope.
method: The authors use the generalized Gross-Pitaevskii equation (gGPE) for large-scale dynamics and the Truncated Wigner Approximation (TWA) to incorporate quantum fluctuations, supplemented by Bogoliubov-de Gennes stability analysis.
model_or_system: A two-dimensional driven-dissipative Bose-Hubbard lattice subject to a single-photon drive, a linear Stark potential (detuning ramp), and a spatially graded loss rate.
key_observables: Local density, total particle number, center-of-mass, generalized imbalance, transverse spectra, and front width.
important_parameters: Stark tilt (F), dissipation gradient (gamma), hopping strength (J), interaction strength (U), and drive strength (Omega).
main_assumptions: The TWA is assumed to be valid in the large-occupation regime, and the 2D dynamics are analyzed using a 1D reduction for the pinned-front regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of the model; Figure 3 presents global and high-resolution phase diagrams in the (F, gamma) plane, illustrating the staircase structure and particle number transitions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the model and the concept of dissipation-selected fronts, develops the theoretical framework for front scaling and stability, presents a phase diagram of dynamical regimes, and concludes with the implications for interface engineering.
why_interesting: It demonstrates how engineered dissipation can act as a control knob for creating and stabilizing complex nonequilibrium structures like interfaces and transport barriers in bosonic lattices.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Setup and density front formation in a two- dimensional driven-dissipative Bose-Hubbard lattice. (a) Schematic of the model. The system consists of an array of nonlinear resonators with on-site interaction U, driven by a single-photon field Ωat frequency ωd. While the system remains uniform along the y-axis, it varies along the x-axis. Specifically, the linear Stark potential Fxj induces the detun- ing ramp, while the dissipation gradient κj = κ0 + γxj sets the spatially graded loss rate. Bosons hop between nearest neighbors at rate J. (b) Time evolution of the condensate density, showing the formation and pinning of the front (red dashed line). Brighter colors indicate higher local...

FIG. 2. Long-time density profiles and transverse spectra at Jt = 500 for fixed dissipation gradient γ = 0.133 and baseline loss rate κ0 = 0.05, with the detuning-ramp slope F set by the Stark tilt. (a) A uniform pinned front at F = 0.31, with a dominant ky = 0 spectral component. (b) A pattern-locked front at F = 0.402, with discrete transverse harmonics. (c) A spatiotemporally chaotic state at F = 0.425, with broadband spectral mixing. (d) A minimum-loss-localized state at F = 0.49, again dominated by ky = 0. (e) Corresponding time evolution of the total particle number N for various F.

FIG. 4. Generalized imbalance I mapped in the plane of detuning-ramp slope and dissipation-gradient strength, (F, γ). (a) and (b) correspond to the low- and high-γ sectors shown in Figs.3(d, b), respectively. The imbalance landscape reproduces the intricate structure observed in the center-of- mass diagram, confirming the sharp boundaries between the pinned, chaotic, and minimum-loss-localized phases. Notably, the discrete terraces are clearly resolved, providing evidence that the staircase depinning and dynamical transitions are in- trinsic features of the system.

FIG. 3. Dynamical phase diagram of the dissipation-selected front in the plane of detuning-ramp slope and dissipation- gradient strength, (F, γ), characterized by the center of mass. (a) Global phase map revealing sharp transition boundaries. (b, d) High-resolution scans of the (b) high-F, high-γ and (d) small-F, small-γ sectors. These diagrams delineate three distinct phases: I (pinned), II (chaotic), and III (minimum- loss-localized). The pinned phase manifests a characteristic staircase structure, which is progressively smoothed by in- creasing dissipation gradient γ.
